data.title: HYD: Solid High-Yield Bond ETF, But Better Choices Out There (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I've written several articles on the VanEck High Yield Muni ETF (BATS:HYD) in the past. I've been bullish, due to the fund's good tax-advantaged 4.4% dividend yield. HYD has slightly underperformed since the most recent one, due to somewhat above-average losses earlier in the year, due to the ongoing events in the Middle East. At the same time, I've recently become aware of the Franklin Dynamic Municipal Bond ETF (FLMI), another muni bond ETF, but one with stronger past performance and higher credit quality, albeit lower 4.0% dividend yield. Overall, HYD seems like a weaker investment than FLMI, so I would not be investing in the former at the present time.

HYD - Overview and Comparison

Strategy and Portfolio

HYD is a simple high-yield muni bond ETF, tracking the ICE Broad High Yield Crossover Municipal Index. Said index all dollar-denominated, tax-exempt, municipal bonds in the market, subject to a basic set of inclusion and exclusion criteria. HYD invests in almost 1,900 different securities, from over a dozen states and several industries.

FLMI, my preferred choice right now, is somewhat smaller, with investments in only 1,100 securities, but with broadly similar industry and state diversification. FLMI is active, which increases risks, but also potential returns and income.

Risk and Volatility

High-yield bond ETFs almost always focus on, well, high-yield bonds, but that is rarely the case for high-yield muni bond ETFs. HYD itself holds a mix of investment-grade, high-yield bonds, and those without ratings. Excluding unrated bonds, HYD focuses on quality investment-grade securities. Although we can't know for certain the quality of unrated securities, these are generally of lower quality, which seems likely to be the case here (impossible to be certain though). Figures are as follows:

FLMI more clearly focuses on investment-grade securities, with single-digit investments in high-yield bonds, a bit under 20% on unrated securities. FLMI is quite clearly the higher-quality ETF, although the unrated bonds complicate the analysis a bit.

In my opinion, and considering the above, HYD should see moderate losses during downturns and recessions, underperforming FLMI and most other high-quality bond ETFs. HYD suffered a 14% NAV drawdown during the pandemic, underperforming FLMI and broader muni bond index ETFs too. Drawdowns were much higher on a price basis, as the fund's discount widened for a couple of days. ETFs rarely trade at a discount, sometimes do so during periods of significant market stress, including during the pandemic. Discounts lasted for a couple of days, in the vast majority of cases.

HYD's duration of 7.3 years is slightly higher than that of most bonds, slightly higher than FLMI's 6.9 figure. Expect greater exposure to rates, slightly higher losses when rates increase, gains when these decrease. As an example, HYD's share price declined by 16.9% from early 2022 to late 2024, during which rates skyrocketed to combat inflation. Losses were around twice those of FLMI, at 8.9%.

HYD's performance was worse than expected: losses were close to twice those of its peer, difference in duration is closer to 6%. Tighter credit spreads explain some of the discrepancy here, perhaps some alpha on FLMI's part.

HYD's greater duration increases long-term risk and volatility and is a net negative for the fund and its shareholders. The extra duration might lead to outperformance if rates were to significantly decline from here on out, a distinct possibility. Due remember that bond prices are rarely directly or tightly linked to Federal Reserve rates, with market sentiment and pricing playing a decisive role for most bonds, including muni bonds.

HYD's greater credit risk is a more straightforward negative for investors, and one without clear upsides. Arguably, the fund could see higher gains if sentiment improves or credit spreads tighten, but gains here are much less directly exposed to credit ratings than above (AA-rated bonds can benefit from tighter credit spreads just as much as those rated B).

Dividend Yield and Performance Track-Record

Riskier bonds and bond ETFs tend to have higher yields than safer, higher-quality alternatives, as is the case for HYD and FLMI. Spreads are tight though, with HYD's 4.4% dividend yield being only slightly higher than FLMI's 3.9% yield.

Spreads are wider for SEC yields, of 4.5% for HYD and 3.7% for FLMI.

Both ETFs pay a monthly tax-advantaged dividend, free of federal income taxes.

Spreads are due to differences in credit quality: HYD focuses on riskier bonds, hence has the higher dividend yield. From prior coverage, spreads widened these past few months, likely due to the ongoing events in the Middle East / bearish sentiment.

HYD's higher dividend yield should lead to higher long-term total returns, but differences in portfolio characteristics and FLMI's active management could end up outweighing the differences in income, especially considering how small these are. FLMI has significantly outperformed HYD since inception, performing quite a bit better than expected:

A significant chunk of the outperformance above was due to FLMI's lower duration, as evidenced by its particularly strong results since 2022. Results were weak prior to that, as rates declined during and before the pandemic.

Although differences in duration do explain a significant chunk of the results above, they don't explain the entirety of these. Specifically, there are several time periods in which rates are flat, even down, during which HYD has still underperformed. As an example, corporate bond rates are down by around 0.20% these past three years:

HYD has the higher duration, so benefitted more from the above. Nevertheless, the fund still underperformed FLMI, and quite significantly:

Credit spreads can't explain these results either, as these are down for these past three years too:

Overall, conditions have been more favorable to HYD than FLMI these past three years, and the fund has still managed to underperform. Results are consistent with FLMI generating a fair amount of alpha, which seems like the likeliest explanation. To me, at least.

Overall Assessment

HYD compares unfavorably to FLMI on most metrics. Credit and rate risk are both a bit higher, drawdowns significantly so. Performance is weaker too, since inception and during most time periods, even when macro conditions are favorable. Risk-adjusted returns are, understandably, weaker too:

HYD's main advantage relative to FLMI is its slightly higher dividend yield, of 4.4% versus 4.0%, but the difference is a bit small and hasn't led to outperformance in the past. FLMI's active management is something of a risk and could be thought of as a disadvantage insofar as it could lead to losses and underperformance. That hasn't been the case in the past, and I have no reason to believe it will be the case moving forward.

In my opinion, and considering the above, FLMI seems like the broadly superior investment. Said conclusion does partly rest on the past performance of both ETFs, and past performance is not guarantee of future results. FLMI could easily start underperforming from here on out, due to ineffective strategies or subpar security selection. I somewhat dislike making these calls based on past performance, but FLMI's track-record is consistently good, and risks are small regardless.

Conclusion

HYD is a simple high-yield muni bond ETF. Although there is nothing significantly wrong with the fund, it compares unfavorably to its peer FLMI on most key metrics. As such, I would not be investing in the fund at the present time.

data.title: GOVZ: A Buy As 30-Year Yields Inch Close To 5%
data.description:
Introduction

U.S. 30-year government bond yields (US30Y) have increased in recent weeks as war in the Middle East pushes back the timing of Fed policy normalization from 2026 to 2027-2028. In fact, as I write this 30-year yields stand at 4.97%, only fractionally below a decade high of 5.09% set in October 2023.

Back then the Fed funds rate was 5.33%, roughly 1.5% above current levels. In essence, we have witnessed a dramatic steepening of the yield curve over the last two and a half years.

One way to position for a flattening of the yield curve, coupled with an outright decline in long-term interest rates, is the iShares 25+ year treasury STRIPS bond ETF (GOVZ). The bullish investment thesis can be summarized as:

  • GOVZ should deliver an attractive low double-digit capital gain for investors with a horizon in late 2027/early 2028.
  • In the meantime, GOVZ offers an attractive mid-single-digit dividend yield.
  • Economic data in 2026 is quite different from 2023, indicating that risks to the GOVZ investment case are manageable.

Strategy and Key Characteristics

GOVZ invests exclusively in STRIPS with a maturity of at least 25 years issued by the U.S. treasury. For those not familiar with STRIPS, think of it as separating the principal and interest payment of a bond in two distinct securities. GOVZ invests primarily in the principal portion of STRIPS, giving the ETF high sensitivity to changes in long term interest rates.

This is shown by the ETF's elevated effective duration of 26.52x, indicating that a 1% move in interest rates will result in a circa 26.52% change in the value of GOVZ's portfolio.

If interest rates increase, this can be disastrous for GOVZ's value, as evident by the ~64% slump in GOVZ price since its inception in September 2020. Back then 30-year yields stood at about 1.45%, roughly 3.5% below current levels. As such, the ETF is not suitable for investors who cannot handle interest rate risk.

GOVZ charges a low 0.10% expense ratio, although that is set to increase to 0.15% in February 2029 once a fee waiver expires. Either way, I see the ETF as a solid bond allocation pick for buy-and-hold passive investors.

Differences between 2023 and 2026

With 30-year yields only marginally below decade highs set in October 2023, it is important to note key differences behind the inflation and unemployment backdrop, as these two economic indicators are key to setting Fed interest rate policy.

The unemployment rate stood at 3.9% in October 2023, somewhat below the 4.2% expected by the Federal Reserve over the long term. As such, a tight labor market warranted a restrictive Fed policy. In contrast, the unemployment rate stood at 4.4% in February 2026, indicating the need for an accommodative Fed stance.

Turning to inflation, in October 2023 inflation was 3.2% Y/Y, underscoring the need for a restrictive Fed policy stance. Here there are some similarities, as the Fed expects inflation to accelerate from 2.4% in February 2026 to about 2.7% for 2026 as a whole.

To sum up, the previous peak in 30-year yields was characterized by a tight labor market and inflation running above 3%. In contrast, 2026 is likely to be characterized by a weak labor market and inflation running somewhat below 3%. As such, there is no reason to believe there will be material tightening in the Fed policy stance, as evidenced by Fed funds futures which point to no change in policy by December 2026.

Why are yields rising

Against this backdrop, one may ask why are 30-year yields so high. I see several driving factors. Firstly, inflation may end up being higher than the 2.7% projected by the Federal Reserve. This in turn may lead to actual rate hikes, although the odds of the Fed funds rate reaching 5.25-5.50% are rather low in my opinion.

Secondly, equity markets have posted losses in recent weeks, leading to some rebalancing between bonds and equities. For instance, even though GOVZ is down around 1.5% on a total return basis so far in 2026, this is still better than the circa 6.75% decline for the Vanguard 500 index fund ETF (VOO) which tracks the S&P 500. As such, some investors are clearly tapping liquidity offered by bond markets to purchase shares.

Thirdly, investors in 30-year bonds in particular may be concerned by the effect a prolonged war in the Middle East will have on U.S. budget deficits and the national debt. For instance, the War on Terror which followed the 9/11 2001 attacks proved to be a turning point for the U.S. economy, at least as far as debt/GDP is concerned. In Q2 2001, U.S. government debt accounted for only 54% of GDP, a level last seen in the early 1990s. The ratio kept increasing in the run up to the 2008 financial crisis, reaching 62% in Q2 2007 before shooting up in the following years. It currently stands at 122% as of Q4 2025.

To conclude, there are several factors contributing to the move in 30-year yields, respectively the recent performance of GOVZ, some of which are short-term, while others may play out over several years.

GOVZ Rating

I believe that as equity markets volatily diminishes, the need to sell bonds to buy stocks will fade out, at least in the short term. As such, one near-term factor putting pressure on GOVZ is likely to disappear. As far as energy prices and their effect on inflation is concerned, it is hard to say how things develop. We may get de-escalation in Iran over the next weeks or months, or the situation may morph into a protracted war such as the one in Ukraine.

Either way, people are already adapting and looking for alternatives, principally renewable energy or coal. As such, I do expect energy prices to peak in the near term, with the pace of declines admittedly uncertain. Even so, I think it is safe to say that the energy price peak will begin to fade out of year-over-year inflation comparisons by Q2 2027, allowing the Fed to resume rate cuts in late 2027 and 2028.

I estimate that this should lead to a circa 0.5-0.6% decline in long-term yields, which together with GOVZ's effective duration of 26.52x could result in a small double-digit gain for investors. For example, a 0.5% decline in interest rates should deliver a gain of ~13.26% for investors. Coupled with the ETF's dividend yield of about 5.12%, this should result in an attractive total return for investors with a horizon on late 2027/early 2028.

As such, notwithstanding risks which I will discuss in the next section, I rank GOVZ with a Buy rating.

Risks

The principal risk to consider is that interest rates may continue to move higher, resulting in further losses for GOVZ. This may be driven by Fed rate hikes, persistently high inflation, or worries about the sustainability of the U.S. fiscal trajectory. Indeed one may well argue that the U.S. economy is on a weak footing, as a budget deficit of 5.77% in 2025 only produced GDP growth of 2.2% last year.

The other key risk to consider is that even if interest rates decline, the purchasing power of fixed income investments such as GOVZ may diminish as well. In essence, a prolonged period of inflation may undermine potential gains one may achieve by investing in GOVZ, even if the bull case develops as expected.

Conclusion

Amid high energy price volatility, the GOVZ ETF has posted a modest year-to-date decline, impacted by a rise in long-term U.S. treasury yields. I believe this presents a buying opportunity for investors with a horizon on late 2027/early 2028, allowing them to achieve attractive total returns, principally driven by an expected decline in long-term treasury yields, complemented by GOVZ's mid-single-digit dividend yield.

A further increase in treasury yields or loss of purchasing power in the event of a prolonged period of inflation are key risks in the GOVZ investment case.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: BlackRock High Equity Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 4.51% (Institutional shares) and 4.45% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2025.

• The largest contributor to relative performance was security selection in the financials, consumer discretionary, and consumer staples sectors. The largest detractor was investment decisions in the communication services, health care, and energy sectors.

• The largest exposures were in the financials, industrials, and health care sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the industrials and information technology sectors, and reduced its exposures to the health care and energy sectors.

Contributors

Stock selection in the financials sector, particularly in the banks industry, was the largest contributor to relative performance. Selection decisions in the consumer discretionary sector, notably in the specialty retail industry, added value. Selection decisions in the consumer staples sector, especially in the consumer staples distribution & retail industry, also had a positive impact.

Detractors

The largest detractor was investment decisions in the communication services sector, particularly in the interactive media & services industry. Security selection in the health care sector, especially in the life sciences tools & services industry, weighed on relative performance. Stock selection in the energy sector, notably in the oil, gas & consumable fuels industry, had a negative impact.

Further insight

After a standout year for U.S. equities in 2025, we enter 2026 constructive but more cautious. Exuberance driven by artificial intelligence (AI) and narrow leadership raises questions about durability, and we think market enthusiasm may be overlooking attractive opportunities beyond AI. At the same time, we see upside risks to inflation from sticky services, tight labor markets, potential energy and commodity volatility, tariff and reshoring pressures, and large-scale AI-related capital spending that could strain power, materials, and construction capacity. These dynamics argue for selectivity and disciplined risk control. In this environment, value investing offers a risk-aware way to participate in secular themes, including AI.

data.title: VPL: Why The Pullback In Asia Pacific Stocks Is A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Asian equities collectively trade near 13x forward EPS estimates. After reaching the mid-teens just a month ago, stocks in the Far East are attractive on valuation, in my view. Hence, I reiterate a buy rating on the Vanguard Pacific Stock Index ETF. I was bullish on the fund back in July of 2025, and it has returned 19.4% since then, total return, outperforming the S&P 500 by almost 20 percentage points.

Shares arguably turned frothy in late February, but now in a 13% drawdown off the 52-week high, a buy-the-dip strategy appears to be a favorable risk/reward play. Today, I’ll provide an updated fundamental look at VPL and refresh the technical outlook.

Asian Stocks Pulling Back on Valuation

Higher Oil Has Weighed on VPL in March

According to the issuer, VPL tracks the performance of the FTSE Developed Asia Pacific All Cap Index, which measures the investment return of stocks issued by companies located in the major markets of the Pacific region. The fund holds stocks of companies located in Japan (the major index component), Australia, Hong Kong, New Zealand, and Singapore, and employs a passively managed, full-replication approach.

VPL is a medium-sized ETF, with $8.9 billion in assets under management as of March 27, 2026. That’s surprisingly down from $10.8 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low at just 7 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 3.82%--more than 2.5 percentage points more than that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum is decent right now, but it has certainly trended weaker over the past four weeks, currently with a B ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Risk levels are in check, but we have seen a notable uptick in volatility. In fact, current VPL implied volatility is 31% (close to that of the SPX).

Finally, on liquidity, VPL’s average daily volume is high at 1.9 million shares, while its median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at only two basis points, per Vanguard.

For an update on the portfolio, the Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar plots along the top row of the style box, indicating its high weighting to large-cap APAC stocks. There’s a nice balance between value and growth, while 21% of the product is classified as SMID-cap.

As of the end of February, the P/E ratio was 15.3x, but after a sharp 12.7% decline in March, the multiple has compressed to about 13x (consider that EPS estimates have actually trended higher). The resulting PEG ratio is quite attractive at less than 1.2x, in light of the 11.26% long-term EPS growth rate.

VPL: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector view, Industrials is the biggest weight. And that’s a problem with Brent oil trading north of $110 per barrel. Japan and other nations rely on the Middle East for energy, so the longer the war in Iran wears on, the more we could see VPL's weakness.

Geopolitical tensions are a clear risk, and so too are rising interest rates. The jump up in Far East yields may weigh on corporate profitability, but it could eventually help some large banks. Indeed, Financials is the second-biggest weight in VPL. Lastly, the memory/storage trade is all the more important, as the ETF’s largest two holdings are Samsung and SK Hynix.

VPL: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal perspective, VPL has tended to do well from March through August. So, we are in the heart of a bullish period, the current March plunge notwithstanding.

VPL: Bullish Seasonality Ahead

The Technical Take

With the ETF back to trading with a favorable earnings multiple amid correction territory, VPL’s technical situation offers an opportunity amid the selloff. Notice in the chart below that shares are encroaching on key support at the $92 mark. That was the high from late last year, and that's where the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of the April 2025 to February 2026 rally comes into play. Moreover, the long-term 200-day moving average will soon be right there, offering a confluence of major support.

Also take a look at the slope of the 200dma—it’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. All of this price action comes after VPL reached technical overbought conditions earlier this year, and I’m not concerned about the RSI momentum oscillator’s range between 30 and 50. For now, $92 is support, while resistance may come into the picture at the flattening 50dma ($101).

VPL: A Confluence of Support in Play Near $92

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on VPL. Asia Pacific stocks look compelling once again on valuation after a steep March reset. Technical support is clearly in view just below the current price with bullish calendar trends ahead.

data.title: Preferreds / Bond Weekly Review: EIC Goes Off-Market With New Preferreds
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our Preferreds Market Weekly Review, where we discuss preferred stock and baby bond market activity from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual news and events, as well as top-down, providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the third week of March.

Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the closed-end fund ("CEF") markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

Preferreds were down on the week and month. Credit spreads across different sectors, including preferreds, widened over the past month, which, together with rising Treasury yields, created a drag on returns.

Preferreds spreads remain relatively tight in the context of the last five years or so.

As a result of rising Treasury yields and wider credit spreads, preferreds yields have risen close to their post-2024 high level.

Market Commentary

CLO CEF EIC launched a public offering of its Series AA and AB convertible perpetual preferreds with coupons of 6%, replacing their exchange-traded term Series C and Series B preferreds. It doesn’t appear that the new preferreds will trade on an exchange, unfortunately, though it’s not a big loss given the coupons are not at all appealing.

Perpetual preferreds are much less appealing than term preferreds because there is no obligation for the issuer to repay "par". Both interest rate and credit curves are upward sloping, so, all else equal, a perpetual preferred would enjoy a higher coupon than a term preferred, which is not what we are seeing with these convertible perpetual preferreds. The low coupon may be explained by the conversion option; however, EIC is not exactly a growth stock, so the value of the conversion can't be that high.

Also in the CLO CEF sector, OXLC priced its preferred at an 8.25% coupon. Grey market/WI ticker is OXFRV, while the permanent ticker will be OXLCM. Maturity will be in 2031. It remains an odd feature of CLO CEF senior securities that the preferreds are not trading at much of a premium to the bonds. For example, two of OXLC bonds are trading at a yield north of 8% just as it is issuing a preferred at 8.25%. We should expect at least a 1% yield premium on the preferreds given their subordination to the bonds in the capital structure.

Mortgage REIT TWO received an offer competing with UWMC. The offer is for $10.70 in cash. The stock jumped 13% during the day. The offer is 4% below the book value as of last quarter. After a $25m termination fee payment to UWMC, there wouldn’t be much left in terms of additional portfolio value to the acquirer. The offer will be evaluated by TWO board. If it is deemed a superior proposal, UWMC will have 3 days to negotiate with TWO and to propose any revisions to its own proposal.

There was a question about the junior subordinated 2062 bond KMPB from insurer Kemper. It’s fairly unusual in that it has a reset structure rather than the typical bullet profile. On its first call date in 2027, it will reset to a coupon of 5-year Treasury yield + 4.14%, unless redeemed. That works out to a 9.1% yield at the current price. A senior 2032 bond of the company is trading at a yield of around 6.3%.

The issuer, Kemper Corp., has low investment-grade senior unsecured ratings from the major rating agencies. KMPB itself appears to be rated BB/Ba1. The market doesn’t think the bond will be redeemed, as it’s trading around $22.40. It’s more than likely that the price will get a bump from additional interest once it resets and the yield jumps. Alternatively, the redemption will be a big one-off gain.

There was also a question about ARES.PR.B, a preferred issued by Ares Management, a large alternative investment manager that also runs ARDC and ARCC. Alternative manager shares have fallen from their peaks on the fallout from private credit newsflow. The fear is that these managers will lose a big chunk of their assets under management and, hence, management fees, either through redemptions or outright credit losses.

ARES.PR.B is a mandatory convertible preferred with the conversion date in late 2027. This means it’s going to trade much like the common, i.e., it doesn’t really make sense to analyze it on its yield profile because that yield is temporary. The total return of the stock will be largely determined by what happens with the ARES common price.

Each ARES.PR.B preferred will be converted into 0.326 shares of the common stock (unless there is a massive rally in ARES, in which case the ratio will be lower). Ultimately, it’s only worth buying for investors who are fairly bullish on the common stock. It is unlikely that investors who like today’s yield of ARES-B will want to stick around with the common shares given their much lower dividend yield. This means they will likely want to exit post-conversion, which then means that where the common shares are trading is essential to the analysis.

For now we remain cautious on the broader preferreds sector due to the steady drift higher in both Treasury yields and credit spreads. Our previous allocations to floating-rate and CMT-reset stocks have worked fairly well, as these shares have struggled less in the current market due to the updrift in longer-term interest rates.

data.title: This Market Is So Up And Down, My Hedges Are Hedged
data.description:
Before I get into the practical part of the article title, I want to talk about trying to buy the ultimate bottom. For some reason, many new or even somewhat practiced managers of their own portfolios are perfectionists when it comes to buying at the ultimate bottom and selling at the ultimate top. While this is a very worthy goal, humans never attain perfection in any endeavor. There is such an occurrence, but it is exceedingly rare, and in most of those 1 in a million cases, it is mostly happenstance when it all comes together. Perhaps in mathematics or Newtonian physics, perfection is achievable. However, in the real physical world conditions, expecting perfection is only going to cause you anxiety or worse. The same is certainly true when it comes to stock investing.

Everyone is looking at their portfolio, even those with the tightest hedges, and avoiding riskier stocks; you will still have plenty of positions that are down 10% or even more. Please bear in mind that this is a moment in time, and stocks move up more than they fall. Self-remonstration is not only futile, but it is also damaging to one's peace of mind. Even worse, it will lead to poor investment decisions. Poor investment decisions will lead to increased anxiety, which in turn will only perpetuate more poor decisions. Once the conditions turn favorable or there is a hint that a favorable situation is coming, stocks will shoot higher.

However, if you choose to have a longer time horizon, let's say six months or a bit more, I am pretty certain that we will see a strong recovery. And that we will see new highs that will take us above 7000, and perhaps as much as 10% higher. If you tightly hold onto the notion of doom, of severe recession, even the dread stagflation, which so many talk about but haven't actually lived through, then you might have already sold out most of your stocks. This article has come too late for you. If you are leaning in this direction, I hope this article will convince you to do otherwise. Before you do something drastic, step back and take a break from watching the market and your portfolio; perhaps after a time you will feel differently.

If even after my last article (cite), you are still questioning your investment choice of staying in this admittedly frustrating avocation, obsession, or occupation, maybe you'll appreciate some further reinforcement of being an active market participant. Even though I am confident that staying in good stocks right now is the right thing to do, I myself look at my waning portfolio balance and feel a pang of loss, even though the logical side of my brain is firmly defeating the reactive side's howls of discomfort. A good friend of mine voiced concern that even with hedging, he is now flat for the year. I don't think it was a brag; my immediate response was that flat was the new up! If you can find ways to protect your downside, perhaps tighten your holdings.

The End is Nigh (in a Good Way)

Hold onto your best names, and by best, I mean names that are growing revenue and cash flow, or even smaller names that have the same and are in a strong growth sector; trim the more speculative ones. I am not saying sell them out completely, just trim down the expensive shares. I probably said this last week: at this point, hold on to that cash until you see that turn in sentiment. I will talk about my hedging in more detail towards the end. The Nasdaq Composite closed at 20948.36, which is now -15.71% from the highs. We are likely at the lows or not very far off. How can I say this when the price of diesel and gasoline is moving so high? Early October 2024, according to AAA, was $3.21. As of March 26, the price was 3.98, 77 cents more expensive, which is, of course, higher but not enough to throw the US into a permanent bear market now, because it didn't back then (cite). In fact, if you recall, 2024 ended up over 23% (cite). This country lived through $90 oil and even higher before and lived. The last time WTI oil rose above 90 was in 2023, actually above $94 per barrel (cite). Guess how high the S&P 500 was in 2023. If you guess over 24%, you'd be correct (cite). Strong growth unaffected by the price of oil.

You Say I Am Comparing Apples to oranges; I'd Say You Are Correct

You'd be correct, but not in the way you think I am agreeing. You'll say that the whole market was brought to you by the Magnificent 7, and we don't have that now. It is true, the M7 has been lagging for a chunk of 2025 until today, yet we are only down 15.71%. Think about it, the stock market has been up a combined 24.2% for 2024, 23.3% for 2024, and 16% for 2025; just nominally, without compounding, that is a combined 64.21%! Much of those gains were either directly attached to AI or adjacent to it. Much of the gains this year before the war were without the M7. In fact, I think some of the biggest bargains now are among those names. That's right, I think the M7 will begin to assert themselves as more and more enterprises embrace generative AI. So we are now in the process of consolidating all the gains of the last 3 years, and as AI adoption grows, companies that demonstrate using the power of AI will also bring attention back to the Magnificent 7. Let's be patient and let the market process the unknown of fuel prices and consolidate the huge gains of the prior 3 years.

Some of you are thinking, "We know from history that the price of oil this high will eventually throw us into recession." Perhaps if oil is trapped over $120 for 6 months, we could have a recession, though I don't think we'll get up and stay up that high anyway. Also, the peak of 2022 with the inflation rate is not the same as 125 and higher of 2022. Here is the chart I have been working off.

The highs of 2022 WTI Oil were the start of the current Ukraine and Russian War at the end of February 2022. It wasn't caused by an actual oil shortage, but the fear of the oil shortage and other fears of a rising belligerence from the east that caused poor performance in 2022. All the armchair historians are pointing to what happened in that year as proof that it will happen this year. Most of the prices of crude here in the US are also about fear of shortages. We are not at all like Europe or Asia, where there is a true shortage developing.

The performance for 2022 was a negative 18%. I will restate that we are currently at -15.76%. To me, this means we are close to the bottom. Back to the difference between 2022 and 2026? Well, the signature bill of the current administration, the BBB (big beautiful bill), is going to pad the salary workers' tax refunds and lower their taxes going forward. The weakest part of the consumer demographic is the hourly and low-level salary worker, so this will offset the cost of gasoline. Also, as we have seen for years, peaks in oil are nearly always followed by a sharp drop in prices, and more oil is produced, which always encourages higher prices. As far as stocks, earnings have never been better. Yes, higher costs might ding Q1 earnings. Two positive factors to note: 1) if the scarcity of oil has more visibility as to when the shortage will be over, and 2) selecting stocks that have little to do with crude oil. The fear of future lower profits will buoy stocks. As for asset-light stocks uninvolved with the price of fuel, picking the right stocks matters. Productivity growth has been offsetting higher input costs, and there is no reason to think that accelerating growth will stop even if the last read was lower. AI is already raising productivity, and that will only grow stronger (cite). In 2022, no one except the researcher was really talking about generative AI, and no one was paying attention yet.

The Ukraine-Russia War is Very Different From the Current War With Iran

The world cannot and will not tolerate this situation much longer. Also, the leadership of Iran has been decimated, and the Iranian people could be slowly starting to take back their country (cite). I am not going to go any further, but the stated goal of the allies is regime change. Even before that, an international naval escort may be announced. I am citing one such article, but there is a stream of articles and statements from various countries that give me a strong conviction that this must happen (cite). I don't want to expend much more text on the geopolitical situation. Just let me end with this: Iran doesn't have the support of the populace, like Ukraine. Russia is not the US or even Israel when it comes to airpower, US naval power, nor does it have the deep intelligence. The command and control that these two allies have. Iran's recent tough talk is just that—talk. That is all the mullahs and the IRGC have left; they will soon run out of missiles and the ability to make them.

Finally, don't discount the power of business to adjust and find ways, both technological and tactical, to lower the cost of transportation. Also, at the end of this article, I will share a company that has been making an alternative fuel that can be mixed with gasoline. I am not going to say this is a long-term investment, but it is likely to do pretty well in the next 3 to 6 months. Not that this crisis will last that long. I started a position premarket only this Friday because I remembered what the company did from past readings. It shot up 10% from where I bought it; it is too small a position to sell

Let's Review and Tie Up Any Loose Ends

Last week, I wrote about recency bias, the notion that the way things are at the moment is the way things will stay. We are all in the thrall of recency, including myself. I try to be self-aware enough to read my own attitude, but it is a full-time job. So let's meet this common distortion head-on.

  • This conflict will end shortly. Either the regime will collapse and be overthrown, or, as necessity is the mother of invention, businesses. Technology, or infrastructure, will find a way

  • A modern economy like the US, which does not have to rely on foreign sources of oil, can still grow on $90 oil. The US also has plentiful Natural Gas, so power generation in the US will continue to grow and stay relatively reasonable price-wise.

  • Our companies are the best in the world at leveraging technology and will keep stock valuations high.

  • At one point, it was feared that all 20M barrels of daily oil would be trapped behind in the Persian Gulf, behind the Strait of Hormuz. Now we find that Saudi Arabia has a pipeline that will soon be capable of reaching 7M barrels a day. That is still a 13M deficit, but it is more than a third less of a deficit now.

  • Many commentators are writing off the US fracking industry to meet demand, but at this $90 to $100 price, many shut-in wells (DUCs) that were too expensive to run will be opened up again.

  • We have the lowest-priced NatGas anywhere. NatGas is a feedstock for many chemical products, one of which can replace gasoline, at least in part.

  • Much of the rise in oil at the moment is fear-driven. If enough practical solutions like the Saudi East-West pipeline are confirmed, the price of oil will drop.

  • Finally, the big rise in the value of stocks in 2024, in spite of WTI trading above $95 a barrel, it follows that this market can brush off the rise and provide stellar results after the shock of suddenly seeing oil at 95ish. I know that WTI oil went above 100 on Friday, but that was out of fear of what could happen over the weekend.

  • Because of the above points and the fact that the Nasdaq Composite nearly fell 16% from the 52-week high, I believe that we are very close to a bottom.

If We Are, as I Believe, Near the Bottom, Here Are Some Sage Words of Baron Rothschild

"The time to buy is when there's blood in the streets, even if the blood is your own."

"Buy to the sound of cannons, sell to the sound of trumpets."

There are many such sayings attributed to N.M. Rothschild, but none are confirmed by his own writings. I believe that they are still very good advice. Now is the chance to buy the best names. I recently added to what I believe is the most magnificent of the "Seven," and that is Alphabet (GOOGL); their generative AI Gemini is, in my opinion, the best of the lot. In fact, I wanted to focus today's article on GOOGL with a catchy title of "Which is Better, Anthropic or ChatGPT? If you answered Gemini 3, you'd be correct. Or something like that. There are claims back and forth, but the general chatter right now is that it is the best (cite). Anecdotally, I have friends who have dumped ChatGPT for Gemini 3. Yes, it is a very small statistical sample, so take this as just one man's opinion. Let's think of it this way: there was a time, perhaps a year ago, that everyone thought GOOGL was a dead man walking because it was losing its Search market share. Now GOOGL is firing on all cylinders. So when GOOGL fell to 276, about a 20% discount, I started buying again, and I will continue to buy at lower levels from here.

Another name I am itching to buy more of is Boeing (BA). Yes, Jet A is expensive right now. However, higher oil prices are just another reason to buy the 737 MAX since it gets much better fuel economy than the older-generation airliners. Also, it is raising its output, which means accelerating growth. I just started to buy a handful, and if it breaks down into the 180s, I will gleefully add more.

What is the Mystery Stock?

I am talking about Methanex (MEOH). I haven't had cause to think of this name for more than a decade, and I don't understand the ways of the mind. I can't remember where I last saw my glasses, 2 minutes ago, and right now I have no idea where my cellphone is, but here we are with MEOH. MEOH takes natural gas as a feedstock and turns it into the simplest alcohol, methanol. Methanol used to be called wood alcohol because the old process used wood to produce it. It is highly toxic in any appreciable amount, but it works just like ethanol and can be mixed with gasoline. Isn't natural gas expensive? Not in North America, which has been going under and over $3 for more than a year, while in Europe the price is well above $10. Don't take my publishing of this stock as an invitation to buy it now. It could just as easily give up this pop as WTI plummets back, once the notion of some spectacular military maneuver that was rumored to take place over the weekend did not happen. I'd better check the news, but earlier today I didn't see any Marines taking over Karg Island. Nope, just checked, and nothing about US ground troops. Maybe we'll see a nice rally tomorrow

Now for the Hedge of My Hedge

In the past few weeks, I've decided to use the 3X inverse leverage Nasdaq-100 (SQQQ). It is less leveraged than using Put options alone. The reason why I like it is that I can close out or add to my SQQQ before the market opens or after the market closes. You can't do that with options, and if the news flow changes in favor of lower oil prices, I would like to close out that position in the pre- or post-market. As the week wore on and my SQQQ position got good profits, I decided to do something new for a hedge tactic. It's an experiment; I did it for one tranche of SQQQ. I wrote a short call against 100 of my SQQQ at the 85 strike with an April expiration. So my thinking is, this market will continue to Yo-Yo like it has been the past 4 weeks. Then there is a good chance that at some point SQQQ will retreat, but I am not anxious to close out the hedge and want to preserve the gains that I made on the hedge. I picked this expiration for the fast theta decay. That means when April 17 rolls around, the Short Call will lose the premium that I received.

That's the plan; let's see what happens. I will report on how it goes. Have a great week, everyone!

data.title: AVUS: A Middle-Ground Sector Exposure With Attractive Valuation
data.description:
The Avantis US Equity ETF (AVUS) is an actively managed fund trading at reasonable valuations, thanks to its stock-picking process and somewhat conservative sector mix. With increased allocation to larger companies and the technology sector since my previous article covering this fund from late 2024, the fund also offers healthy profitability levels, despite lagging broader equity indexes like the Russell 3000. This paints a mixed picture for the fund at this moment, as described in the following sections.

ETF Description & Highlights

AVUS is an actively managed ETF that offers broad exposure to U.S. companies of all sizes. Through a research-driven process, the fund relies on the ability of its portfolio managers to identify companies that trade below their value and with a high profitability profile. To achieve this, the fund employs metrics such as the book-to-price ratio to determine companies’ valuation, while profitability is primarily calculated based on cash flow from operations to book value. As a final step, companies are weighted according to their respective market capitalization, adjusted for their value and profitability, where those with more attractive characteristics are assigned a heavier weight in the portfolio.

As of February 28, 2026, AVUS managed approximately $10.9 billion, allocated across roughly 1,909 holdings, with an average market capitalization of $174.1 billion, which is considerably lower than the Russell 3000, a total market capitalization index, and AVUS's preferred benchmark. While the fund’s allocation is well diversified across all market caps, mega caps remain the largest category with 34.7% of assets, followed by large caps at 27.5% and mid-caps at 24.3%. This represents only a modest shift from the allocation profile seen in November 2024, when mega- and large-caps accounted for 31.3% and 25.9% of the fund, respectively, suggesting a growing preference for more established companies rather than small- and mid-caps, which have been under a relatively challenging environment due to elevated interest rates.

AVUS’s top ten holdings are NVIDIA (NVDA) (4.9%), Apple (AAPL) (4.85%), Microsoft (MSFT) (3.67%), Amazon (AMZN) (3.23%), Alphabet A (GOOGL) (2.49%), Meta Platforms (META) (2.33%), Alphabet C (GOOG) (1.98%), JPMorgan (JPM) (1.33%), Exxon Mobil (XOM) (1.30%), and Micron Technology (MU) (1.19%), which account for nearly 27% of the fund. While many of these top holdings are the same as broader equity indexes, their weights are slightly lower in the fund, even compared to the Russell 3000, reinforcing its diversified approach.

The table below includes six ETFs that also employ a total market stock selection approach, mixing fundamentals with dynamic allocation, which will serve as the peer group for AVUS in the analysis. The first is the DFA Dimensional US Equity Market ETF (DFUS), which favors smaller and profitable companies. A somewhat different angle comes from the ActivePassive U.S. Equity ETF (APUE), which blends active managers with a passive methodology tied to the CRSP U.S. Total Market Index, forming a hybrid structure. The JPMorgan Equity Focus ETF (JPEF) takes a more concentrated route, holding a relatively small portfolio selected through fundamental research. Meanwhile, the DoubleLine Shiller CAPE U.S. Equities ETF (CAPE) relies on a valuation-driven sector rotation, allocating capital based on the Shiller CAPE, which allocates the portfolio to the cheapest sectors of the S&P 500. The Invesco Russell 1000 Dynamic Multifactor ETF (OMFL) tracks a rules-based index derived from the Russell 1000, dynamically adjusting weights across value, momentum, quality, low volatility, and size factors. Finally, the iShares US Equity Factor Rotation Active ETF (DYNF) allocates dynamically following a methodology that adopts momentum, quality, value, size, or minimum volatility factors, according to the short-term outlook.

From a sector allocation perspective, AVUS leans most heavily into technology (25.6%), though being somewhat lighter than the benchmark. Financial services (15.4%) is the second-largest sector, followed by a fairly balanced mix of consumer discretionary (11.9%), industrials (11.5%), and communication services (9.9%). The fund still has relevant exposure to energy (7.8%) and healthcare (7.8%), while smaller positions include consumer staples (4.7%), basic materials (2.6%), and utilities (2.6%). Overall, the fund has increased its exposure to technology (+2.7%) and communication services (+1.4%) since late 2024, which gives it a stronger growth profile. Compared to the Russell 3000, though, AVUS remains a value-oriented fund in general, given its overweight positions in energy (+3.7%), followed by financial services (+2.8%), though also in the usually high-multiple consumer discretionary (+1.9%) as well. At the same time, an underweight stance in technology (-6.0%) also helps pull valuation down.

Breaking down the fund’s portfolio, we see that in technology, the exposure looks broad at first glance, with nearly 200 holdings, but the bulk of the allocation is concentrated in a few names, such as Nvidia, Micron, and Broadcom (AVGO) in semiconductors, and Microsoft representing the largest holdings in software, with marginal positions in Oracle (ORCL) and Palantir (PLTR). Beyond these two big segments, Apple is the only significant position.

In financial services, the sector is even more diversified with almost 400 holdings, yet core positions are limited to sub-industries, including global banks, led by JPMorgan, Wells Fargo (WFC), and Bank of America (BAC), while Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), and American Express (AXP) add exposure to global payments. In addition, smaller positions in Goldman Sachs (GS), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), and insurers like Progressive (PGR) and Travelers (TRV) complete the sector exposure.

Holdings across the equity market that are worth mentioning include Alphabet, Meta Platforms, energy majors Exxon Mobil and Chevron (CVX), along with industrial players, such as Caterpillar (CAT), GE Aerospace (GE), and RTX (RTX). Still, Amazon is by far the largest position in consumer discretionary, followed by a marginal allocation to carmakers General Motors (GM) and Ford (F), as well as retailers like TJX (TJX) and Ulta Beauty (ULTA). Still, holdings in pharmaceuticals like Eli Lilly (LLY), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and Merck (MRK), and hypermarkets Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST) round out the fund’s allocation across the equity market.

As we can see, despite holding nearly 2000 stocks, much of AVUS’s allocation is concentrated in mega caps. This reflects the fund’s weighted methodology, which is structured based on market capitalization, adjusted by value and profitability. As these large companies, especially big techs, also exhibit some of the highest margins in the stock market, it appears to me that names like NVIDIA, Alphabet, and Microsoft should continue to anchor AVUS’s portfolio unless there is a clear shift in the fund’s strategy. This is expected to give the fund some resilience, given the financial strength of these companies, while its overweight stance in energy, although modest at 3.7%, is expected to serve as an inflation hedge, as much of the inflation concerns at this moment are tied to an extended surge in oil prices.

AVUS is overweight in sectors like energy, industrials, consumer discretionary, and financial services relative to the peer group. This mix does not change much of the fund’s profile compared to peers, as technology remains the largest sector for all peers, with the exception of CAPE, due to its distinct sector selection process. That said, as expected, AVUS’s sector correlation remains high with all peers and above the 0.90 level, except CAPE, as these funds appear to avoid overconcentration in defensive sectors, as some value-oriented funds do, favoring a middle-ground approach, much like AVUS.

Trading at a Discount to Peers and the Broader Market

AVUS’s 19.2x P/E represents roughly an 11% discount to the Russell 3000’s 21.6x. This gap is in part driven by the fund’s sector mix, due to its underweight exposure to technology and greater allocation in value sectors like energy and financial services. But the stock selection plays a role as well, as most sectors trade at a discount to the benchmark, particularly consumer discretionary and industrials.

At the same time, the fund shows slightly softer growth metrics relative to the broader market. Its historical earnings growth of 3.1% falls short of the Russell 3000’s 7.6%, while sales growth of 6.1% is also modestly below the benchmark’s 6.6%, largely driven by weaker exposure to a growth area like technology. A similar pattern is seen in profitability. AVUS’s EBITDA margin (31.9%) trails the Russell 3000’s 33.6% by 1.7 percentage points, while its ROE of 38.6% also lags the benchmark’s 39.5%. The sector mix continues to play a key role, as overweight sectors like industrials and energy do not have margins as high as technology. This is only partially offset by stock selection in sectors like technology, healthcare, and consumer discretionary, where operational efficiency, as measured by ROE, is much better than in the Russell 3000.

That said, there is a mixed scenario in place. On the one hand, AVUS’s sector mix helps maintain disciplined valuation, but the flip side is that growth and profitability metrics fall short of broader equity indexes. Still, the gap is relatively small, given significant positions in high-margin big tech names like NVIDIA, Alphabet, and Meta Platforms. However, it appears to me that investors in general are likely to expect more from a fund that has a value and profitability mandate. Anyway, as we will see later in the article, the fund’s track record points to performance in line with the Russell, despite some inconsistency in terms of yearly returns.

AVUS trades at the lower end of the peer group’s valuation range. With a P/E above the Russell 3000’s 21.6x, funds like DFUS, JPEF, and DYNF’s premium valuations reflect a combination of greater exposure to technology and mega caps. That gives AVUS a relative advantage in a market that still looks at least fairly valued if we consider high expectations already priced into many companies right before the current selloff.

Performance in Line With the Russell 3000

The fund’s annualized returns of 18.1% (3-year) and 10.9% (5-year) are roughly in line with the Russell 3000. This comes as AVUS has done well in 2021 and also in 2022 during the market correction, which is something that the fund is repeating now, with a strong outperformance in 2026. On the other hand, AVUS lagged the broader market in the bullish years of 2023 and 2024, likely driven by its somewhat sector mix. The fund also shows a mixed picture compared to peers, with a mid-range performance over the 3- and 5-year windows. Despite the obvious limitations in this comparison due to new funds like APUE and CAPE, we can say that AVUS has lagged behind top performers such as JPEF and DYNF, as these have outpaced the broader market with strategies like portfolio concentration and dynamic allocation.

AVUS has also seen volatility in line with the broader market, with beta in the 1.00-1.04 range, while peers like CAPE, OMFL, and DYNF have kept beta below 1.00. That said, while AVUS has done better than the benchmark in key bearish environments, the reality is that its downside capture ratio in the 102-116 range, well above most peers, suggests some vulnerability during declines.

With an average daily volume of $36.35 million, AVUS is a liquid fund, though it still falls short of larger peers like DYNF and DFUS, which trade much higher volumes. Its 0.03% bid-ask spread remains tight and among the lowest in the group. The fund’s turnover of just 2% is quite low, which helps keep the expense ratio at a reasonable level and below the peer group average. On the income side, the 1.04% yield is the second-highest in the group, as most peers exhibit relatively low yields. Meanwhile, the fund’s tracking error in the 0.84%-0.90% range is in the middle of the pack, and its Sharpe ratios of 1.10 (3-year) and 0.67 (5-year) are close to the peer group average but below DFUS, JPEF, and DYNF, as those funds have delivered a better combination of returns and volatility.

Upside Drivers and Risk Considerations

From a macro perspective, my read is that AVUS tends to hold up well on a relative basis if inflation concerns lead to a “higher for longer” environment, as financial services, a sector the fund is overweight in, should see higher margins with elevated interest rates, as long as credit conditions remain okay. In addition, energy, another sector where AVUS is overweight, is likely to benefit from higher oil prices in the event of a prolonged conflict in the Middle East.

At the same time, a slowdown in economic growth would likely pressure cyclical sectors such as industrials and consumer discretionary, leaving the fund somewhat exposed. That said, the opposite scenario cannot be dismissed. If risk appetite strengthens at some point, the market could once again be led by growth sectors like technology, which is an area where AVUS remains structurally underweight, despite its core positions in some big tech names. In such a scenario, the fund should see positive returns, but it is unlikely to keep up with broader market equity indexes.

Overall, AVUS’s middle-ground positioning suggests it may navigate different environments with relative stability, but it is unlikely to be a clear outperformer unless we see a sustained rotation toward value sectors.

Final Thoughts

Trading below the Russell 3000 and most peers, AVUS’s valuation remains attractive, driven by disciplined stock selection and a conservative sector mix. On the other hand, while profitability is not bad, being just slightly lower than the Russell 3000, it may disappoint investors seeking quality exposure. Still, in addition to the macro considerations in the previous section of this article, with worries about inflation pressures tied to oil prices still alive, the fund’s tilt toward value and its core exposure to some of the largest big tech names are expected to offer some cushion at this moment. Given this mixed outlook, I keep a neutral stance on this fund for the time being, while investors looking for opportunities to outperform the broader market may find better risk-reward alternatives elsewhere. Despite that, for those already positioned in the fund, a total return in the high-single-digit range over the next twelve months seems reasonable, assuming double-digit earnings growth in the S&P 500 in 2026, as projected by the financial market.

data.title: Tracking The $13.61B Lone Pine Capital Portfolio - Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Lone Pine Capital's 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/17/2026. Please visit our Tracking Stephen Mandel's Lone Pine Capital Portfolio article for an idea of their investment philosophy and our previous update for the fund's moves during Q3 2025.

Note: Stephen Mandel stepped down from managing investments at Lone Pine Capital in January 2019 in a previously announced (September 2017) move. He is currently a managing director at the firm. data.title: Invesco Global Allocation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The fund's developed ex-US tactical factor equities were a positive contributor

The fund's allocation to Invesco International Developed Dynamic Multifactor ETF (IMFL) (IMFL) was one of the top contributors to relative return for the quarter, driven by its cyclical factor positioning.

2 The fund's macro framework experienced a regime change in the fourth quarter

The fund was positioned for a contraction regime and shifted to a recovery regime in November, driven by improving growth expectations. In our view, the transition does not represent the start of a new cycle, but an extension of the existing cycle.

3 Tactical foreign exchange activity detracted from relative performance

Tactical foreign exchange activity was the largest detractor from relative performance. Allocations to US and emerging market equities also detracted.

Manager perspective and outlook

• Global economic growth held up in the fourth quarter and markets ended higher despite volatility. Risk assets rallied early but stalled in November amid apparent uncertainty about US Federal Reserve (Fed) policy and artificial intelligence (AI) related valuation concerns. December saw renewed strength in US, European and emerging (ex-China) equities. Credit markets faced isolated pressures from bankruptcies and regional banks but still had gains.

• Geopolitics appeared to influence investor sentiment. US/China tensions rose over rare earth minerals and tariffs before easing after a temporary trade deal. The US government shutdown appeared to add uncertainty as data releases were paused. Central banks were active: the Fed cut rates twice and raised its 2026 growth outlook, the Bank of England lowered rates and the European Central Bank raised growth and inflation forecasts.

• We expect growth to reaccelerate in 2026, supported by lower US policy rates and more fiscal spending across major economies. Private sector balance sheets have remained a tailwind. We believe Fed easing should support US growth later in the year, while less aggressive easing abroad points in our view to a weaker dollar and improving opportunities outside the US, including emerging market local debt. In Europe, we believe fiscal expansion and real wage gains should bolster activity. Given elevated US valuations, we favor broadening exposures to cyclicals.

Portfolio positioning

Invesco Solutions' macro regime framework remained in a recovery regime entering the first quarter. Global growth has been broadening, with improvements in non-US developed markets, and inflation has continued to moderate across regions. This environment should in our view support broadening of equity performance across sectors and regions.

We maintain overall portfolio risk above the benchmark , with an overweight in equities relative to fixed income, tilting toward value and small- and mid-capitalizations. Regional equity exposure is in line with the benchmark. In fixed income, we maintain an overweight in credit risk and a neutral duration stance.

Within equities , we maintain overweight exposure in cyclical sectors, value and small- and mid-cap stocks because these equity segments carry higher operating leverage and historically tend to outperform during cyclical rebounds. Accordingly, we favor sectors such as financials, industrials, materials and energy at the expense of health care, consumer staples, utilities and technology. We kept the fund's regional composition in line with the benchmark given mixed signals among key drivers of relative performance between US, developed ex-US and emerging market equities. On one hand, US earnings momentum has continued to outperform other markets. On the other hand, our expectations for US dollar depreciation, driven by narrowing yield differentials and positive global growth surprises, favor international equities. As a result, we express no active views in the fund's regional exposures at this stage.

In fixed income , we maintain an overweight in credit. However, with spreads near all-time lows, we believe the case for taking additional credit risk is limited to harvesting higher yields. As a result, we seek diversification across high-yield, leveraged loans and emerging markets dollar debt, while underweighting investment grade credit and sovereign fixed income. Our bearish positioning on the US dollar also favors emerging market local debt and global fixed income, currency unhedged, over core US fixed income. We maintain an overweight in Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) relative to nominal Treasuries following the transition to a recovery regime, which has historically been accompanied by rising expectations for future inflation.

Performance highlights

Invesco Global Allocation Fund Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) had a positive return in the fourth quarter and outperformed its blended benchmark, the Custom Invesco Global Allocation Index.

The fund was positioned for a contraction regime and shifted to a recovery regime in November, driven by improving growth expectations. In our view, the transition does not represent the beginning of a new cycle, but rather an extension of the existing cycle, given tight labor markets and credit spreads between Treasury and non-Treasury sectors being near all-time lows.

Following the transition of our macro framework to a cyclical recovery, we implemented several changes to Invesco Global Allocation Fund. We increased overall portfolio risk above the benchmark, moving to an overweight in equities relative to fixed income and tilting toward value and small- and mid-capitalization stocks. Our increased equity exposure -- particularly the shift toward mid and small cap stocks -- has allowed us to rotate into areas of the market that we believe offer more attractive valuations. As part of this repositioning, we reduced exposure to expensive defensive sectors, which have been trading at an average forward P/E of about 19.5x. We then reallocated toward more historically economically sensitive (cyclical) sectors, where valuations appear considerably lower, with an average forward P/E closer to 8.5x.

In fixed income, we increased credit risk to an overweight relative to the benchmark and reduced duration back to neutral following the decline in bond yields. We continued to underweight the US dollar, driven by narrowing yield differentials relative to the rest of the world and positive economic data surprises outside the US.

Contributors to performance

From a top-down allocation perspective, the primary contributors to relative return were an underweight in international fixed income relative to the benchmark and an allocation to developed ex-US tactical factor equities.

Detractors from performance

From a top-down allocation perspective, the primary detractors from relative return were allocations to US large-cap and emerging market equities.

The fund's foreign exchange activity was a detractor from relative performance.

data.title: Janus Henderson Small-Mid Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 2.47% and the Russell 2500™ Value Index returned 3.15%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the information technology and industrials sectors detracted from relative performance, while stock selection in the materials and real estate sectors contributed.

Outlook

We remain confident in our disciplined value investment approach even as we see potential sources of market volatility.

Investment environment

• Stocks rose on positive earnings news and expectations for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts. The Fed followed up a September rate cut with two 25 basis-point (bp) rate cuts, in October and December, respectively.

• Given the protracted government shutdown, economic data was sparse except for company commentary and private investment surveys, which pointed to a slowly improving economic environment.

• While investors paid more attention to fundamentals relative to earlier in the year, we continued to see pockets of outperformance by lower-quality stocks, as non-earners continued to outperform.

Portfolio review

Relative performance was hindered by our position in Bath & Body Works (BBWI). The specialty retailer reported disappointing revenue growth and reduced guidance, reflecting both cautious spending by its consumers as well as some operational issues that need to be addressed. Despite an inexpensive valuation, we have meaningfully reduced our holdings given our concerns over poor execution by company management, and because we believe the next iteration of the company's turnaround will take considerable time.

Detractors in the industrials sector included Janus International Group (JBI), a manufacturer of industrial doors and storage units for the self-storage providers. Demand for self-storage space has remained anemic due to sluggish home sales and slower housing turnover. Against this backdrop, Janus International reported disappointing quarterly results. We added to the position on weakness.

Owens Corning (OC) was another detractor. We invested in the roofing and building products supplier because of its attractive valuation, which we believed amply priced in the risk of continued weakness in new home starts. However, a less severe storm season also resulted in fewer roof repairs, further dampening the company's revenue growth. Given near-term uncertainty for the business, we exited the position.

Kirby Corporation (KEX) was a positive contributor. Kirby transports petroleum and petrochemical products. It also operates an engine overhaul business, which has seen strong growth due to the rapid buildout in U.S. data center capacity. After underperforming in the third quarter owing to weaker petrochemical volumes, the stock moved higher in the fourth quarter, as the company became viewed as an AI beneficiary. We continue to hold a sizeable position in Kirby.

Globus Medical (GMED) was a relative contributor in the healthcare sector. The medical device-maker delivered strong execution and improved profitability, supported by synergies from recent acquisitions. Robust sales of spinal products resulted in better-than-expected third-quarter earnings growth and an improved outlook.

Information technology company Teradyne (TER) was another positive contributor, as continued rapid investment AI and other advanced computing drove demand for its semiconductor testing equipment. The company is also priced at a lower valuation relative to other AI-related stocks, and we believe it provides an attractive avenue for capitalizing on the AI buildout.

The portfolio ended the quarter overweight in the financials, information technology, materials, and energy sectors. It was underweight in the communication services, industrials, healthcare, real estate, utilities, consumer staples, and consumer discretionary sectors.

Manager outlook

We remain cautiously optimistic about the market in 2026. The economy continues to grow at a steady pace, and we may see modest improvement in cyclical sectors such as transportation, industrials, and materials. While weaker employment growth and above-average inflation have pressured consumer spending by lower-income households, this slowdown has been offset by resilient spending from higher-income households benefiting from equity price appreciation. We also see potential for additional Fed rate cuts and fiscal stimulus that could provide a tailwind for economic growth.

At the same time, many companies remain cautious in their hiring and capital spending plans. Valuations in the equity market do not appear cheap by historical standards, but we have continued to find areas of opportunity, such as in the healthcare sector. Recently, the market has experienced increased volatility around the AI trade: on some days, excitement around AI drives market returns, while on other days investor concerns lead to a recalibration. We would expect this dynamic to continue as we head into 2026.

As always, we are on the lookout for opportunities to use volatility to our advantage as we seek out high-quality opportunities yet to be recognized by other investors. We remain focused on investing in well-managed, profitable companies with low debt levels that we believe are undervalued by the market. In our view, such companies can outperform regardless of the economic backdrop.

We thank you for your continued co-investment in the Janus Henderson Small-Mid Cap Value Fund (JVSIX).
